title: Eduversal AI Prompt Library
subtitle: Tested, role-aware copy-paste prompts for classroom, leadership, and specialist work — maintained across the academic year

audience._note: Every prompt carries an audience field indicating who it's primarily for. Filter the library in the reader UI by your role to surface relevant prompts first; the rest remains discoverable.

_meta.purpose: A living prompt library that an educator returns to dozens of times across an academic year. Every prompt is structured, copy-paste-ready, tested against the 4-part mega-prompt pattern (Role + Context + Task + Constraints), and anchored to a workflow in a role playbook.
_meta.referenceLayer: ../reference/ — verbatim Eduversal v1.0 + UNESCO + external sources.
_meta.doNotFabricate: Every prompt carries provenance. Prompts marked 'practitionerPattern' come from confirmed 2025-2026 sources cited in manifest.externalAnchors.practitionerPatterns2026. Prompts marked 'eduversalSynthesis' are derived from Eduversal v1.0 framework expectations and are explicitly flagged as not yet field-validated.

howToUseThisLibrary.browsing: Prompts are organised by TASK CATEGORY first (lessonPlanning, differentiation, etc.), then by SPECIFICITY within each category (general-purpose → subject-specific → edge-case). Browse by category when you have a task type in mind. Use the reader UI's role filter to surface prompts most relevant to your role.
howToUseThisLibrary.copyAndAdapt: Every prompt has three parts: (a) the COPY-PASTE BLOCK — what you paste into your AI tool, with [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS] for you to fill in; (b) NOTES — what to expect, common pitfalls, and what to ask for in follow-up turns; (c) PROVENANCE — where this prompt pattern comes from.
howToUseThisLibrary.iterating: First-turn output is rarely the final answer. Every prompt includes 'followUpTurns' suggestions — the 2-3 follow-up prompts that consistently improve output. AI Practitioner-level work uses iteration; Foundation-level work often stops at turn 1 and gets generic results.
howToUseThisLibrary.subjectAdaptation: Many prompts have a 'subjectVariants' section with calibration notes per subject area (math, English, science, humanities, Bahasa Indonesia). Subject-specific advice changes the underlying advice; not optional flavour text.
howToUseThisLibrary.redLines: Every prompt links to relevant redFlags from the role playbooks. If a prompt's intended use violates a red flag (e.g. entering PII), the prompt is annotated with a 'NEVER' warning. Don't ignore these.
howToUseThisLibrary.bookmark: Bookmark this page. The library is meant to be reopened weekly across the academic year as you encounter new tasks. New prompts and refinements are added each term — see versionHistory at the bottom.

promptingPrinciples._purpose: The same principles apply to every prompt in this library. Reading these once saves time on every prompt later.

explanation.role: Tell the AI who it should act as. 'You are an experienced Year 8 mathematics teacher preparing for a Cambridge Lower Secondary class.' This single sentence shifts output quality dramatically.
explanation.context: Give the AI what it needs to know about your situation: subject, year group, syllabus reference, class profile, prior lesson, what you're trying to achieve. Specifics here = specifics in output.
explanation.task: State what you want, concretely. 'Draft a 50-minute lesson on…' not 'help me plan a lesson on…'
explanation.constraints: Tell the AI what to avoid: 'No Western cultural assumptions — use Indonesian contexts.' 'Maintain Cambridge AO alignment.' 'No worked examples — students should derive themselves.'

iterateDontOneShot.title: Iterate, don't one-shot
iterateDontOneShot.explanation: First-turn output is the AI's default best guess. Follow-up turns are where quality emerges. After the first output: ask for refinement, ask for variations, ask for what's missing, ask for misconception coverage. Practitioner-level AI use is conversation, not query.

namedPersonas.title: Be specific about who the output is for
namedPersonas.explanation: Instead of 'students', say 'a class of 28 Year 8 students at a Cambridge International school in Indonesia, 4 EAL students, 1 student with diagnosed dyslexia'. The AI will adjust register, examples, and scaffolding. Generic input = generic output.

alwaysReview.title: AI drafts. You author.
alwaysReview.explanation: Every output from every prompt in this library is a DRAFT. Your professional judgement on what to keep, what to cut, what to rewrite is what makes the final material yours. AI output used unedited reads generic — students and parents notice within days.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — both strong, pick whichever your school has approved.
workWellWithTheseTools.alternative: MagicSchool — has dedicated 'Lesson Plan' tool that may be faster than starting from a general chat.

prompts.title: Lesson plan skeleton (50-90 minute lesson)
prompts.useWhen: Planning any new lesson where you don't have an existing template to adapt
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher at a Cambridge International school in Indonesia.

Context:
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Cambridge syllabus reference: [E.G. 'Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 1112 Stage 8, Unit 3']
- Lesson duration: [MINUTES]
- Position in unit: [LESSON N of N]
- Prior lesson covered: [ONE-SENTENCE SUMMARY]
- Next lesson will cover: [ONE-SENTENCE SUMMARY]
- Class profile: [SIZE, EAL count, SEN notes — anonymised, no names]

Task: Draft a lesson SKELETON, not a finished lesson plan. Include:
1. Two or three learning objective options aligned to the Cambridge AO above
2. Three starter activity ideas (5-10 minutes each)
3. A main activity scaffold (not the full activity, just the structure)
4. Three formative-check questions I can use mid-lesson
5. Two differentiation moves: one for stretch, one for support
6. An exit ticket idea (3-5 minutes)

Constraints:
- Use Indonesian contexts (currency in IDR, place names, situations a Jakarta or Surabaya student recognises) where examples are needed.
- Maintain cognitive demand in differentiation — easier ≠ less thinking.
- Don't write out finished activities. I want a skeleton I will flesh out with my class context.
prompts.expectedOutput: A structured response covering all 6 sections, with placeholders for your specific class context. Plausible Cambridge AO alignment (verify).
followUpTurns: 'Where will Year [N] students typically struggle with this topic? List the top 3 misconceptions and how to surface them.'
'For each of your three starter ideas, what's the cognitive demand level on Bloom's? I want to choose one that pitches at the right level.'
'Give me two alternative main-activity scaffolds — one more discussion-led, one more individual-work-led. I'll pick based on the class energy that day.'
pitfalls: AI sometimes invents plausible-but-wrong Cambridge AO references. Verify against the actual syllabus document.
If your class profile includes named students or specific behavioural data, anonymise before pasting (UU PDP). 'A student with diagnosed dyslexia' is fine; 'Andi who has dyslexia' is not.

subjectVariants.math: Add to constraints: 'For starter ideas, prefer real-world problem framings over abstract drill. For misconception list, prioritise the ones I'd see in IGCSE marking — these are the long-term traps.'
subjectVariants.english: Add to context: '[Reading text / writing genre / language feature being studied]'. Add to constraints: 'Maintain target-language register; don't simplify the model texts to below class level.'
subjectVariants.science: Add to context: '[Practical work component, if any]'. Add to constraints: 'If practical work is involved, specify equipment that a typical Indonesian school lab would have — avoid assuming American-spec lab equipment.'
subjectVariants.humanities: Add to constraints: 'Where contested topics arise, present multiple perspectives including non-Western framings. Indonesian historical and political content must be factually verified, not paraphrased from AI memory.'
subjectVariants.bahasa_indonesia: Add to constraints: 'Write the lesson skeleton in English (for my planning), but flag any Indonesian-language elements that I need to prepare in Bahasa. Don't write the Bahasa elements — I will write them in my own register.'

prompts.title: Unit overview from syllabus reference
prompts.useWhen: Planning a new unit at the start of term, especially if it's a unit you haven't taught before
prompts.timeBudget: 20 minutes, ~10 with AI + 10 reviewing and customising
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher at a Cambridge International school in Indonesia.

Context:
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Cambridge syllabus: [FULL SYLLABUS REFERENCE INCLUDING CODE]
- Unit name and number: [E.G. 'Unit 4: Algebraic Expressions']
- Approximate teaching time: [WEEKS / LESSONS]
- This is my [first / nth] time teaching this unit.

Task: Draft a unit overview including:
1. The big ideas (3-5) that should anchor the whole unit
2. The sequence of sub-topics, with rough lesson allocations
3. Prerequisite knowledge students need going in (so I know what to diagnose in the first lesson)
4. Common misconceptions to watch for across the unit
5. Two or three unit-end assessment ideas aligned to Cambridge AOs
6. Suggested formative checks at the end of each sub-topic

Constraints:
- Reference Cambridge syllabus AOs explicitly — I will verify the alignment.
- Suggest where I might invest extra time vs. move quickly, based on typical Year [N] difficulty patterns.
- Identify any sub-topic where practical / project work would land better than direct instruction.
prompts.expectedOutput: Structured unit overview, 600-1000 words. Suggested sequences are starting points, not final plans.
followUpTurns: 'For each common misconception, give me one quick diagnostic question I could use in the first lesson to identify which students hold it.'
'Of the unit-end assessment ideas, which would best align with the Cambridge AO weightings for this syllabus? Cite the AO codes.'
'Where in this unit would AI-assisted student practice be appropriate, and where would it undermine learning?'
pitfalls: AI's Cambridge AO citations are unreliable — always verify against the live syllabus.
AI's lesson-allocation suggestions are based on generic difficulty assumptions. Your class's pacing reality will differ.

differentiation.categoryNote: Differentiation is the #1 teacher AI use case in 2025-2026 (~61% per Anthropic's educator usage report). It's where the highest-leverage, lowest-risk AI work happens for most teachers.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: MagicSchool AI — dedicated differentiation tools including leveled text rewriter (the standout tool in this category).
workWellWithTheseTools.alternative: Diffit (paste any source text/URL → leveled versions) or Brisk Teaching (Chrome extension inside Google Docs).
workWellWithTheseTools.generalPurpose: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers also work — slower than specialised tools but flexible for unusual differentiation requests.

prompts.title: Three-version practice (access / on-level / stretch)
prompts.useWhen: You have a practice set ready for the on-level student and want access + stretch versions
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher.

Context:
- I have a practice set designed for on-level Year [N] students (pasted below).
- I need three versions: access (for students who need scaffolding), on-level (refine my version), stretch (for students who finish quickly and need rigour, not just more work).
- Class context: [CLASS SIZE, EAL count, SEN notes — anonymised]

My on-level set:
[PASTE YOUR PRACTICE SET HERE]

Task: Produce three versions with the same number of items.

For the access version: add scaffolding (worked-example hints embedded, sentence starters, vocabulary support). Don't reduce cognitive demand — make it accessible while preserving the thinking required.

For the on-level version: review and refine my original. Sharpen wording, fix any ambiguity, ensure progression within the set.

For the stretch version: increase cognitive demand, not just difficulty of numbers or vocabulary. Use transfer questions, novel contexts, open-ended extensions. Specify which Bloom's level each stretch item targets.

Constraints:
- Use Indonesian contexts for any new examples.
- Maintain the same learning objective across all three versions.
- Don't water down the access version into trivia — it should still earn the same understanding goal.
prompts.expectedOutput: Three versions of the practice set, with annotations explaining the differentiation decisions in each.
followUpTurns: 'For the access version, identify which scaffolds I could remove progressively across the next 3 lessons so the student moves toward on-level independence.'
'For the stretch version, give me one or two extension questions that would push a student into Cambridge IGCSE-level thinking, even if they're in Year 8.'
'Are there any items in my on-level set that don't actually align to the learning objective? Be specific.'
pitfalls: AI often interprets 'easier' as 'shorter sentences + simpler numbers'. Specify cognitive-demand parity explicitly.
The 'stretch' version sometimes just adds more items rather than harder thinking. Push back if so.

prompts.title: Rephrase complex task for EAL students
prompts.useWhen: A complex worded task in a non-language subject needs to be accessible for EAL students without losing rigour
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher working with EAL students at a Cambridge International school in Indonesia.

Context:
- The task below is for Year [N] students.
- Some students in the class are EAL at intermediate English level (around CEFR B1).
- The COGNITIVE demand of the task should not change. Only the language load should.

Original task:
[PASTE TASK HERE]

Task: Rephrase this for B1-level EAL access. Then provide a brief glossary at the bottom for any term above B1 that the student would need to know.

Constraints:
- Don't simplify the subject vocabulary — students need to learn it. Provide it with a definition, not a substitution.
- Keep instruction verbs precise (analyse, evaluate, justify — don't substitute these with 'think about').
- Glossary entries: maximum 6, only for terms that are non-negotiable subject vocabulary.
followUpTurns: 'Are there any phrases in the rephrased version that are still likely to cause an EAL student to misunderstand the cognitive task? Identify them.'
'For the glossary terms, suggest one quick activity I could use at the start of the lesson to pre-teach them.'
pitfalls: Over-glossing: AI sometimes adds glossary entries for common words. Cut anything obvious.
Subject vocabulary loss: AI sometimes substitutes 'photosynthesis' with 'how plants make food'. That defeats the EAL student's right to learn the actual word.

prompts.title: Misconception-aware practice variations
prompts.useWhen: Practising a topic where students reliably get one specific thing wrong, and you want practice that surfaces (rather than hides) the misconception
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher and item designer.

Context:
- Topic: [TOPIC]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- A common misconception in Year [N] is: [DESCRIBE THE MISCONCEPTION]

Task: Write five practice problems where a student holding this misconception would arrive at a specific predictable wrong answer. For each problem, also write:
- The correct answer
- The misconception-driven wrong answer that the problem is designed to surface
- A feedback prompt I can use with a student who arrives at the wrong answer, designed to surface the misconception so the student can resolve it themselves

Constraints:
- The misconception trap must be genuine — a student NOT holding the misconception should naturally get the right answer.
- Don't write trick questions. The point is to expose thinking, not to mislead students who actually understand.
- Indonesian contexts for any narrative framing.
prompts.expectedOutput: Five problems, each with correct answer, misconception-driven wrong answer, and Socratic feedback prompt.
followUpTurns: 'For one of these problems, walk me through how a student holding the misconception would reason from problem statement to wrong answer. I want to see the path.'

'Give me two more problems where the same misconception leads to a DIFFERENT wrong answer than in problem 1 — I want to test whether students always make the same error or whether it varies by context.'
pitfalls: AI sometimes invents 'misconceptions' that aren't real. If a misconception sounds suspiciously clean, test on a real student first.
The 'feedback prompt' AI generates is often too leading. Edit to make it genuinely Socratic — questions, not answers in disguise.

rubricAndFeedback.categoryNote: Rubric-anchored feedback prompts consistently outperform open feedback prompts. The rubric forces the AI to be specific. Build the rubric well; the feedback follows.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: MagicSchool — Rubric Generator + Report Card Comments + Text Feedback bundled in one tool.
workWellWithTheseTools.googleWorkspaceSchools: Brisk Teaching — inline feedback directly on student Google Docs.
workWellWithTheseTools.soloLowerPrivacyRisk: Eduaide.ai — teacher-only login, simpler privacy footprint.
workWellWithTheseTools.criticalWarning: Tools draft. Teachers mark. Red line 2 applies regardless of which tool you use.

prompts.title: Four-band rubric (Emerging / Developing / Secure / Exceeding)
prompts.useWhen: Setting a new task that you'll mark formatively, especially if you want Cambridge-band-aligned descriptors
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher familiar with Cambridge International assessment bands.

Context:
- Task: [DESCRIBE THE TASK]
- Learning objective: [STATE THE LO]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- This rubric is for FORMATIVE use only — not for grade reporting.

Task: Draft a 4-band rubric with three criteria. Bands are: Emerging, Developing, Secure, Exceeding (aligned to Cambridge band thinking).

For each criterion at each band, write a descriptor of 1-2 sentences describing observable student performance. Avoid vague language ('shows understanding'); be specific about what the student does or produces.

Constraints:
- Three criteria only. More than three becomes unmanageable in classroom use.
- Each descriptor should be observable in actual student work — not internal qualities.
- Progression between bands should be substantive (Secure ≠ Developing + adjective).
- This rubric will be used by a class teacher, not a moderator — keep it usable.
prompts.expectedOutput: A 4-band rubric with 3 criteria, ~12 descriptor cells.
followUpTurns: 'Test this rubric on a hypothetical student work sample at the Developing band. Write the sample (2-3 paragraphs of student-realistic work) and show how the rubric applies.'
'Identify any criterion where the bands feel like a single criterion with adjectives added. Suggest substantive differentiation between bands.'
'For Cambridge IGCSE alignment, what AOs does this rubric implicitly cover? List them — I will verify.'
pitfalls: AI's first draft often has Secure = 'shows good understanding' and Exceeding = 'shows excellent understanding'. Force substantive differentiation.
Don't use this for summative grading without department moderation. The red flag is in the playbook for a reason.

prompts.title: Feedback comment bank from rubric
prompts.useWhen: After a class set submission where you want consistent, rubric-anchored feedback comments to adapt for each student
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher.

Context:
- Rubric: [PASTE YOUR RUBRIC]
- Student work type: [DESCRIBE — e.g. 'short answer historical analysis', 'algebraic problem set with working shown']
- Year group: [YEAR]

Task: For each rubric level (Emerging, Developing, Secure, Exceeding), draft a comment TEMPLATE I can adapt for individual students. Each template should include:
1. One specific strength I can name (with a [PLACEHOLDER] for the student-specific instance)
2. One next-step framed as a question (Socratic, not directive)
3. One concrete action the student can take this week

Constraints:
- Each template ≤ 3 sentences. Comments any longer are not read.
- Avoid generic praise ('good effort'). The strength must be evidence-anchored.
- The next-step question should feel forward-looking, not punitive.
prompts.expectedOutput: Four templates (one per band), each with three placeholders and three concrete elements.
followUpTurns: 'For the Developing-band template, draft three example outputs as if for three different actual student responses. I want to see how the template adapts.'
'Are any of the templates overly similar across bands? Differentiate them more.'
pitfalls: AI-drafted comments must be edited per student. Sending unedited templated comments is detectable by students within 2-3 returns and trust collapses.
The 'next-step question' is often generated as a statement disguised with a question mark. Make it genuinely open.

prompts.title: Hinge questions for mid-lesson formative check
prompts.useWhen: You want a quick, multiple-choice-style question mid-lesson that tells you whether to move on or re-teach
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher familiar with Dylan Wiliam's formative assessment work.

Context:
- Learning objective: [LO]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Most common misconception in this topic: [MISCONCEPTION]
- I will use this question mid-lesson, after initial teaching, to decide whether to move on or re-teach.

Task: Write three hinge questions where:
- Each is answerable in under 30 seconds.
- Each has a single correct answer and 2-3 plausible wrong answers.
- At least one wrong answer surfaces the named misconception above.
- The question is designed so that students who picked different answers can be quickly grouped for differentiated next-step instruction.

For each question, identify:
- The correct answer and why it's correct.
- What each wrong answer reveals about the student's thinking.
- A two-sentence re-teach plan if more than 30% of the class chooses a wrong answer.

Constraints:
- Don't write trick questions.
- The misconception trap must be genuine — a student who actually understands gets it right naturally.
prompts.expectedOutput: Three hinge questions with diagnostic interpretation for each answer choice.
followUpTurns: 'Test question 1 on a hypothetical student who has the misconception. Walk through their reasoning to a wrong answer.'
'For each question, suggest one variation I could use in a different lesson without students recognising the pattern.'
pitfalls: AI sometimes generates 'wrong' answers that are actually defensible. Test each on yourself: would a clever student have a case for the wrong answer?
The 30-second constraint is real. If you have to think for 30 seconds, students will need 60+.

parentCommunication.categoryNote: Parents detect generic AI tone within 1-2 messages. Every prompt here produces a DRAFT that you edit substantially into your own voice. Unedited AI-drafted parent communication is a red flag.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: Claude for Education for tone-aware bilingual drafts — strongest Bahasa register per 2025-2026 benchmark.
workWellWithTheseTools.forTranslation: DeepL Pro — best dedicated translator for EN ↔ Bahasa. Better register than Google Translate.
workWellWithTheseTools.doNotUse: Free Google Translate for high-stakes parent letters — register often wrong. Free ChatGPT or any consumer-tier account when student details are in the prompt (UU PDP).

prompts.title: Bilingual (English + Bahasa Indonesia) parent message
prompts.useWhen: Any non-trivial parent message where bilingual delivery serves the family better — progress concerns, positive recognition, incident notes
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced teacher at a Cambridge International school in Indonesia, drafting a parent message.

Context (no student names — anonymised):
- The student: [DESCRIBE — e.g. 'a Year 7 student in my mathematics class']
- The situation: [DESCRIBE THE REASON FOR THE MESSAGE]
- The tone I want: [warm-but-professional / direct / celebratory]
- The family expectation: [Indonesian parents often expect more formal Bahasa than the LLM defaults to — match formal Bahasa register]

Task: Draft TWO versions: English first, then a Bahasa Indonesia version.

The Bahasa version is not a literal translation — it's a re-rendering for the cultural and linguistic register of Indonesian parent communication. Use 'Bapak/Ibu' formal address. Use Indonesian-appropriate ways of opening (a brief warm greeting before the topic) and closing (a thank-you and contact invitation).

Constraints:
- Maximum 6 sentences per version.
- No technical education jargon (no 'formative assessment', 'differentiation') — use plain language.
- Don't promise outcomes you can't guarantee.
- The English and Bahasa versions should match in content but feel native in each language.
prompts.expectedOutput: Two versions, each ~6 sentences. Bahasa version uses formal Indonesian parent register.
followUpTurns: 'The Bahasa version sounds [too casual / too formal / a bit translation-y] — rewrite it.'
'Add a specific concrete detail at the end that only I as the teacher would know — a placeholder I will fill in.'
pitfalls: Bahasa from default LLMs tends to be either slightly too formal or slightly too casual for parent communication. Read aloud before sending.
Never paste the student's actual name. UU PDP applies.
Always edit. Parents recognise generic AI tone.

prompts.title: School-wide announcement (e.g. AI policy, curriculum change)
prompts.useWhen: Annual or termly announcements to the whole parent body about substantive school-level changes
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are the [PRINCIPAL / ACADEMIC COORDINATOR] of a Cambridge International partner school in Indonesia, writing to the whole parent body.

Context:
- Topic: [E.G. 'introducing our AI integration policy', 'curriculum changes for the new academic year']
- Parent audience: mixed — some are anxious, some are curious, some are ahead of us in their own AI use with their children.
- Length target: ~400 words English + ~400 words Bahasa.
- Tone: confident but honest about uncertainty. Cambridge-aligned framing.

Task: Draft both versions.

Structure:
1. Opening (warm acknowledgement of parents as partners)
2. What we are doing and why (concrete, plain language)
3. What we are NOT doing (address common parent concerns directly)
4. How parents can engage (Q&A session, contact channels)
5. Closing

Constraints:
- Cite Cambridge International and Eduversal as authorities, not your own judgement.
- Acknowledge uncertainty where it exists (e.g. 'this policy will be reviewed annually').
- No promises about outcomes you can't deliver.
- The Bahasa version should feel native, not translated. Use formal parent register throughout.
prompts.expectedOutput: Two ~400-word versions. Structured. Cambridge + Eduversal cited.
followUpTurns: 'The opening feels generic — give me three alternative openings, each more specific to a Cambridge International school in Indonesia.'
'Section 3 (what we are NOT doing) — expand this. Parents need clear reassurance on grading, data privacy, and child safety.'
'Read the Bahasa version aloud to me — does it sound like a school letter or like a translation? Suggest changes.'
pitfalls: School-wide announcements drafted by AI without significant editing sound corporate and patronising. Edit ruthlessly.
If you don't know whether you're doing something, don't claim you are. Honest uncertainty is more credible than performative confidence.

adminAndSummarisation.categoryNote: The lowest-risk, highest-leverage AI use category. Meeting notes, briefing summaries, action item extraction — fast wins with minimal red-flag exposure.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — strong general summarisation.
workWellWithTheseTools.forLiveMeetingTranscription: Otter.ai (Pro tier minimum). Acceptable for general staff meetings. NOT for staff appraisal or safeguarding meetings.
workWellWithTheseTools.forMultiDocumentAnalysis: NotebookLM — load multiple policy documents, Cambridge handbooks, or Permendiknas regs and cross-reference them.

prompts.title: Meeting / PLC / training summary
prompts.useWhen: After any meeting where you've taken rough notes and need a shareable summary
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are summarising meeting notes for me.

Context:
- Meeting type: [DEPARTMENT PLC / STAFF TRAINING / LEADERSHIP MEETING / etc.]
- My role: [ROLE]
- The notes below are my raw, unedited capture from the meeting.

Raw notes:
[PASTE YOUR NOTES]

Task: Produce a structured summary:
1. Three bullet points capturing the key decisions made.
2. A list of action items in the form 'Action: [task] — Owner: [PLACEHOLDER for me to add name] — Due: [date]'.
3. A list of unresolved questions that need follow-up.
4. One sentence on the meeting's overall tone (cooperative / contentious / unclear).

Constraints:
- Don't invent decisions or actions that aren't in my notes.
- If something in my notes is ambiguous, flag it rather than guessing.
- No personal information about named individuals' performance — I will add owner names myself.
followUpTurns: 'For each action item, suggest one realistic first step the owner could take this week.'
'Identify any decision in my notes that might be controversial when communicated to the wider staff. Flag it.'
pitfalls: If notes include discussion of specific staff performance, don't paste them in. Personal-evaluation content doesn't belong in a third-party AI tool.
AI sometimes invents action items that 'should' have been agreed. Strip these out.

prompts.title: Network bulletin / Cambridge update synthesis
prompts.useWhen: A long network bulletin, Cambridge update, or external policy document arrives and you need to extract what matters for your context
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are helping me parse a document for my role.

Context:
- My role: [ROLE]
- My subject(s) (if applicable): [SUBJECTS]
- Document type: [BULLETIN / POLICY / GUIDANCE]
- Source: [ORIGINATING BODY — Cambridge / Eduversal / Indonesian ministry / etc.]

Document:
[PASTE OR ATTACH]

Task: Provide:
1. A 3-bullet summary of the document's main points.
2. A list of changes (if any) from previous versions or current practice, with each change's scope of impact.
3. Actions required by me in my role — with urgency tagged (immediate / this term / this year).
4. Actions required by others I should escalate to.
5. Any claims in the document I should verify against original sources rather than trust the summary for.

Constraints:
- Don't editorialise. Stick to what the document says.
- Flag anything ambiguous rather than resolving with your interpretation.
- For Cambridge documents, your summary is a starting point for me to verify against the original — never the final word.
followUpTurns: 'Of the actions you've identified, which are likely to be most contentious with staff? Suggest framing for communication.'
'Where does this document leave room for school-level interpretation, and where does it not?'
pitfalls: Never propagate AI-summarised Cambridge updates to staff without reading the original. AI summarisation drops nuance that matters.
AI's urgency tagging is its own guess. Validate against your local context.

alsoUsefulFor: subject_leader (when contributing to department-level item drafting)
specialistWork.categoryNote: These prompts touch the assessment boundary. Read specialist-playbook.json#theAssessmentBoundary before using. AI may draft; formal items require human final authoring.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: Claude for Education — most cautious tone, lowest fabrication rate on Indonesian-specific content. Best for curriculum drafting where errors propagate network-wide.
workWellWithTheseTools.forBulkPracticeItemSeeding: Wayground (formerly Quizizz AI) or Questgen — practice-bank only, never formal pipeline. Always specialist-review.
workWellWithTheseTools.forCrossDocumentWork: NotebookLM — Cambridge syllabus + scheme of work + examiner reports in one notebook; ask alignment questions.

prompts.title: MCQ draft for practice bank (NOT for formal pipeline)
prompts.useWhen: Drafting practice-bank items where AI assistance is appropriate. NEVER for chapter_test_items, ease_items, or Cambridge-equivalent items.
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced HQ subject specialist for Cambridge International schools, drafting practice items for student self-study.

Context:
- Subject: [SUBJECT]
- Cambridge syllabus reference: [SYLLABUS + STAGE / IGCSE / etc.]
- Topic: [TOPIC]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Item bank purpose: practice (student self-study, NOT formal assessment).
- A common Year [N] misconception in this topic is: [MISCONCEPTION]

Task: Draft 5 MCQ items. For each item:
- Stem (clear, ungrammatically simple, single answer)
- 4 options: 1 correct, 3 distractors
- Of the 3 distractors, at least 1 should surface a specific misconception (label which)
- The other distractors should be plausible but not trap-style
- Brief rationale for the correct answer
- Brief comment on what each distractor reveals about student thinking

Constraints:
- Indonesian contexts where examples are used (IDR, place names, foods).
- No trick wording.
- Stem should be answerable in under 60 seconds for an on-level student.
- These are PRACTICE items — never formal assessment.
followUpTurns: 'For each item, audit the distractors: are any so implausible that no student would pick them? Suggest replacements.'
'Run a cultural-bias check: is anything in these items assuming Western/American context? Surface and rewrite.'
'Estimate the difficulty of each item for Year [N]. (I'll treat this as a sanity check, not a calibration source.)'
pitfalls: AI distractors sometimes include a defensible 'wrong' answer. Test each.
AI sometimes invents Indonesian-context details that don't exist. Verify any specifics.
NEVER use for formal pipeline. The specialist-playbook red flag rf_specialist_authoring_formal_items applies.

prompts.title: Distractor quality audit for existing items
prompts.useWhen: Auditing imported or older items (Latihan.id imports, IGCSE Tools imports, legacy HQ-authored) for distractor quality
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced item designer reviewing an existing MCQ item for distractor quality.

Context:
- Subject: [SUBJECT]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Source: [LATIHAN.ID / IGCSE TOOLS EXAMVIEW / HQ-AUTHORED / OTHER]
- Use: [PRACTICE BANK / NOT FORMAL ASSESSMENT]

Item:
[PASTE ITEM WITH ALL OPTIONS]

Task: Evaluate each distractor. For each:
1. Is it plausible enough that a student holding a specific misconception would pick it? Name the misconception.
2. Is it implausible (no real student would pick it for principled reasons)?
3. Does it have an unintended interpretation that makes it accidentally defensible?

Then suggest:
- Which distractors to keep as-is.
- Which to revise (specify how).
- Whether the item is overall fit for the practice bank or should be retired.

Constraints:
- Don't rewrite the stem unless you flag a fundamental issue with it.
- Don't change the correct answer.
- Cultural-bias issues count as fitness issues — flag them.
followUpTurns: 'For the distractor you flagged for revision, draft three replacement options. Each should surface a different student-thinking pattern.'
'If this item is being retired, suggest two replacement items targeting the same learning objective with stronger distractors.'
pitfalls: Imported items often have multiple weak distractors. Don't try to revise all in one pass — focus on the worst.
AI sometimes flags culturally appropriate distractors as problematic (false positives). Use your judgement.

anchors: Eduversal monorepo §19 Latihan import quirks documented

policyAndStrategy.categoryNote: Policy AI use is high-risk because AI reads plausibly while missing local regulatory context. Use for structure and first drafts; expert review for substance is non-negotiable.

workWellWithTheseTools.primary: Claude for Education — most cautious of the core three. Tends to flag uncertainty rather than fabricate confident-sounding regulatory detail.
workWellWithTheseTools.forCrossReferencingMultipleSources: NotebookLM — load UU PDP text, Cambridge guidance, Eduversal framework docs in one notebook to draft policy that cites all three correctly.
workWellWithTheseTools.doNotUse: Free / personal AI accounts. Policy work touches confidential institutional content. Free tier = unsafe.

prompts.title: School AI policy first draft
prompts.useWhen: Drafting or substantially revising your school's AI policy. AI for STRUCTURE only; substance requires legal + Cambridge + Eduversal alignment review.
prompts.timeBudget: 60+ minutes for first draft, then weeks of review
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are helping a school leader draft a school-level AI policy.

Context:
- School: Cambridge International partner school in Indonesia, Eduversal network.
- Year groups served: [E.G. 'Primary through Upper Secondary']
- Regulatory environment: UU PDP No. 27/2022 (Indonesia data protection); Permendikdasmen 13/2025 (Koding & AI elective curriculum); Cambridge International coursework AI guidance; Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0.

Task: Draft a school AI policy with these sections:
1. Purpose and scope
2. Approved tools (placeholder for school's specific list)
3. Staff AI use expectations (Foundation level minimum)
4. Student AI use expectations (age-appropriate per Eduversal Part 2)
5. Data protection requirements (UU PDP-aligned)
6. Academic integrity (Cambridge-aligned, especially for coursework)
7. Disclosure norms (when AI use must be declared)
8. Review and revision cycle (annual)

Constraints:
- Cite each external authority (UU PDP, Cambridge, Eduversal) by name where relevant.
- Use plain language — this is for staff and parents, not lawyers.
- Flag clearly any section where local regulatory specifics need to be verified by the school (do NOT invent regulatory detail).
- Maximum 2 pages.
- This is a FIRST DRAFT — explicit caveat that legal review, Cambridge Coordinator review, and Foundation Rep review are required before adoption.
followUpTurns: 'Section 5 (Data Protection) — expand this. UU PDP requirements are non-trivial and the draft is too generic.'
'Section 6 (Academic Integrity) — explicitly reference Cambridge's coursework AI guidance via the Exams Officers Guide Phase 3 framing.'
'Identify any clause in the draft that I should flag to legal counsel before adoption.'
pitfalls: Never adopt an AI-drafted policy as-is. Legal review is mandatory.
AI tends to write policy that reads plausibly while missing UU PDP specifics. Verify every data-protection clause against actual regulatory text.
Don't let AI invent Indonesian regulatory citations. If it cites a specific article number, verify.

studentFacingActivity.categoryNote: Prompts to prepare AI-LITERACY-DEVELOPMENT activities for students. The activities themselves run in classrooms; the prep happens here.

workWellWithTheseTools.forPreparation: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — generate the flawed AI output that students will critique; then your own judgement on which errors to highlight.
workWellWithTheseTools.forLiveClassroomUseWithStudents: SchoolAI (teacher dashboard, supervised) OR Khanmigo (maths) OR Curipod (most conservative for younger years). Never general-public chatbots for live student use.
workWellWithTheseTools.forResourcesAndSlides: Canva for Education + Magic Studio — make accompanying student worksheets, posters, slide decks.

prompts.title: Prepare a 'critique an AI output' lesson
prompts.useWhen: Once per term per class — students examine an AI-generated response in your subject and critique it
prompts.copyPasteBlock:
You are an experienced [SUBJECT] teacher preparing a 'critique AI output' lesson for [YEAR] students.

Context:
- Topic students have been learning: [TOPIC]
- Year group: [YEAR]
- Lesson duration: [MINUTES]
- Goal: students develop the skill of critically evaluating AI-generated subject content.

Task:
1. Generate an AI response to a typical task or question in this topic. The response should be plausible but contain at least 3 errors or weaknesses (factual error, missing nuance, anachronism, cultural bias, or oversimplification).
2. Create a student worksheet that asks them to annotate the response: what's correct, what's wrong, what's missing, what's superficial. Provide guidance questions but not answers.
3. Provide a teacher answer key explaining each of the 3+ errors.
4. Suggest a discussion structure for the lesson's second half (small group → whole class).

Constraints:
- The errors should be subject-realistic, not absurd. Students need to see that AI errors are subtle and require attention.
- Indonesian context where applicable.
- Don't reveal the errors in the worksheet itself.
prompts.expectedOutput: AI response (with hidden errors) + student worksheet + teacher answer key + discussion structure.
followUpTurns: 'One of the errors is too obvious — make it subtler.'
'Add a cultural-bias error that an Indonesian student would notice but a Western student might not.'
'For the discussion, suggest three open questions that move from "what's wrong with this AI output" to "what does this tell us about how to use AI well in this subject".'
pitfalls: AI sometimes refuses to generate intentionally flawed content. If so, prompt: 'This is for a critical-thinking lesson — generate a response that contains realistic AI-style errors, and I will use it to teach students to spot them.'
The 'errors' AI plants are sometimes too easy. Push for genuinely subtle ones.

academicYearCadence._purpose: How to use this library over the rhythm of an academic year — not a one-time read, but a recurring reference.

August_September: Bookmark this library and walk through the categories once. You don't need to absorb all prompts — just know what's where.
Pick one prompt from each of lessonPlanning, differentiation, and rubricAndFeedback to try in your first three weeks.
Set a quarterly calendar reminder to revisit the library for new additions (see versionHistory).

October_December: Settle into a stable AI workflow with 3-5 prompts you use weekly. Don't try to use everything.
If you're a Subject Leader, share two prompts with your department members and ask which worked.
Run one 'critique an AI output' lesson per class before the end of Term 1.

January_March: Revisit prompts you haven't tried yet. Most teachers settle into a comfortable subset by January; expanding past it is where Practitioner-level growth happens.
Check the versionHistory for prompts added since you last looked.
If you're a Subject Leader or Coordinator, audit your department/school's AI prompt patterns informally. Drift is normal; intervene if quality has dropped.

April_June: Reflect: which prompts saved you the most time? Which produced the best student outcomes? Note this for next year.
If you've discovered new prompts that worked well, share them with your Academic Coordinator for possible inclusion in the next version of this library.

pairedDocuments.teacherPlaybook: teacher-playbook.json — every classroom prompt here anchors to a workflow in the teacher playbook
pairedDocuments.leaderPlaybook: leader-playbook.json — leadership-track prompts (policy, parent comms, briefing) anchor here
pairedDocuments.specialistPlaybook: specialist-playbook.json — HQ specialist prompts (item drafting, distractor audit, curriculum) anchor here
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — when a prompt's use is ambiguous, decision-trees clarify
pairedDocuments.redFlagsRedlines: redFlagsAndRedlines.json — every prompt watches relevant red flags via redFlagWatch field

versionHistory._purpose: Track additions and significant changes to this library across the academic year. Educators returning to the library can scan recent additions.

added: Initial release with 7 categories and 13 prompts
Categories: lessonPlanning, differentiation, rubricAndFeedback, parentCommunication, adminAndSummarisation, specialistWork, policyAndStrategy, studentFacingActivity
All prompts include subjectVariants where applicable
knownAbsences: Subject-specific prompts for arts (music, visual art, drama) — to be added Term 2
Pastoral and SEN-specific prompts — deferred pending policy clarification on AI use in safeguarding contexts
Cambridge IGCSE / AS-A Level coursework-specific prompts — pending Cambridge Coordinator review
Bahasa Indonesia language teaching prompts — deferred to Bahasa specialist contribution

contributionInvitation._purpose: This library grows by practitioner contribution. A prompt you tested and that worked is the most valuable thing you can add.
howToContribute: Document the prompt you used (full text), the context, and what you got out of it.
Send to your Academic Coordinator, who forwards to HQ Subject Specialist for review and inclusion.
Contributions credited in versionHistory (anonymised if preferred).
whatGetsIncluded: Prompts that worked across multiple uses, not one-offs.
Prompts where the output clearly reflects the 4-part mega-prompt structure.
Prompts that avoid red flags by design, not by luck.

openQuestions._note: Questions this library intentionally does not answer.
items: Network-level approved-AI-tools list for 2026-2027 — pending. Prompts reference generic tool categories.
Whether Eduversal will produce its own Bahasa Indonesia teaching prompts (specialised for Bahasa as a subject, distinct from bilingual prompts) — open.
Prompts for AI-Lit subject teaching (Permendikdasmen 13/2025 Koding & AI elective) — deferred pending Eduversal adoption decision.
Integration of Cambridge IGCSE / AS-A Level coursework-specific prompts — pending Cambridge Coordinator review of what Cambridge permits.
